coredata.dc:title: Bidirectional heterojunctions photocatalyst engineered with separated dual reduction sites for hydrogen evolution

Wang, Bolong
Bai, Xiao
Liu, Weisheng
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic technology is identified as one of the most clean and sustainable routes for hydrogen generation. Nonetheless, inadequate photoinduced carrier separation limits photocatalytic performance. Rationally designing bidirectional heterojunctions that leverage single/two-photon co-excitation pathways and dual reduction sites, thereby inhibiting recombination of the photoinduced carriers and exposing active sites to increase photocatalytic activity. This study proposes bidirectional heterojunctions consisting of 0D carbon dots (CDs) and nickel phthalocyanines co-modified sulfur-doped multivesicular carbon nitride tubes (denoted as 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %). The as-prepared photocatalyst displays a remarkable yield of 16,363.62 μmol g−1 h−1, with a rate 23.2 times that of pristine carbon nitride (denoted as PCN, 704.23 μmol g−1 h−1). The improvement of photocatalytic performance is attributed to the efficient separation and transportation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs induced by the coexistence of single/two-photon excitation pathways and dual reduction sites. The efficient combination of lateral and vertical heterostructures plays an essential role in the photocatalytic process and is exhaustively studied using catalyst rational design and modification technology.

head.ce:title: Bidirectional heterojunctions photocatalyst engineered with separated dual reduction sites for hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic technology is identified as one of the most clean and sustainable routes for hydrogen generation. Nonetheless, inadequate photoinduced carrier separation limits photocatalytic performance. Rationally designing bidirectional heterojunctions that leverage single/two-photon co-excitation pathways and dual reduction sites, thereby inhibiting recombination of the photoinduced carriers and exposing active sites to increase photocatalytic activity. This study proposes bidirectional heterojunctions consisting of 0D carbon dots (CDs) and nickel phthalocyanines co-modified sulfur-doped multivesicular carbon nitride tubes (denoted as 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %). The as-prepared photocatalyst displays a remarkable yield of 16,363.62 μmol g−1 h−1, with a rate 23.2 times that of pristine carbon nitride (denoted as PCN, 704.23 μmol g−1 h−1). The improvement of photocatalytic performance is attributed to the efficient separation and transportation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs induced by the coexistence of single/two-photon excitation pathways and dual reduction sites. The efficient combination of lateral and vertical heterostructures plays an essential role in the photocatalytic process and is exhaustively studied using catalyst rational design and modification technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 0D carbon dots (CDs) and nickel phthalocyanines co-modified sulfur-doped multivesicular CN tubes (denoted as 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %) were designed and synthesized by in situ supramolecular self-assembly, thermal polymerization of freeze-dried supramolecular aggregates, and the surface hydroxyl group-induced assembly. As-prepared 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % catalyst shows an impressive photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 16,363.62 μmol g−1 h−1, with a rate 23.3 times that of PCN (704.23 μmol g−1 h−1). The constructed one/two-photon excitation pathways and bidirectional transfer mechanism results in dual reduction sites, which can optimize the separation and transfera of photogenerated carriers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic H2 evolution is a promising technology for directly capturing and utilizing solar energy due to its outstanding potential for producing renewable energy and mitigating the greenhouse effect [1–3]. Nevertheless, inefficient utilization of sunlight, high recombination of photogenerated carriers and sluggish surface reaction kinetics adversely affect photocatalytic efficiency [4,5]. The efficient separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and the highly directional transfer of electrons enhance photocatalytic activity. The construction of a one-photon excitation pathway using a semiconductor with wide bandgap or carbon materials can improve the photocatalytic activity, by inhibiting photon-generated carrier recombination, broadening the photo-absorption region, and promoting photon-utilization efficiency [6,7]. For a two-photon excitation pathway, suppression of the continuous electron migration from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) in individual components can be realized via staggered band structure of semiconductor materials [8,9]. Consequently, the construction of bidirectional heterostructure with the coexistence of one/two-photon excitation pathways, including all the above advantages, is undoubtedly highly desired.

Since high thermal/chemical stability and suitable optical/electronic properties, graphite carbon nitride (CN) has emerged as a viable metal-free semiconductor material [10–13]. However, it is still necessary to introduce intrinsic external driving forces to accelerate carrier transfer, while expanding the light absorption. Two-dimensional (2D) lateral heterostructure has high stability and extensibility. The covalent bonds at the interface can act as charge transport channels to promote the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers [14–16]. 2D/2D material seamlessly stitched together in the in-plane could effectively adjust the band structure and electronic transport property, improving electron transport ability along the in-plane direction [16,17]. Additionally, due to the strong absorption in the 550–750 nm range and unique unsaturated-coordinated Me-N4 sites [18–20], 2D metal phthalocyanines (Mepc) have gained significant attention in photocatalysis. The Mepc molecule has uniform and exposes catalytic sites for photocatalytic reaction, owing to a well-defined single-atom metal site with the coordination of four N atoms (Me-N4) [21,22]. However, poor conductivity and aggregation limit its application in photocatalysis. To address these issues, an induced assembly process with a chemical driving force promotes high dispersion, and the thickness of Mepc is controlled [23,24]. At the molecular level, the Mepc and CN vertical heterostructures are constructed by the surface hydroxyl-induced assembly, significantly accelerating charge transfer kinetics and extending visible-light absorption [25,26]. Therefore, it is feasible that the surface hydroxylation of CN controls the assembly of Mepc via the H-bonding formation. Enlightened by the previous study, Ni–N4 centers of nickel phthalocyanines (Nipc) could function as the catalytic sites for evolving H2 [27,28]. Designing bidirectional heterojunctions is highly desirable and unprecedented but challenging to achieve the coexistence of one/two-photon excitation pathways.

In this study, 0D carbon dots (CDs) and nickel phthalocyanines co-modified sulfur-doped multivesicular CN tubes (denoted as 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %) were synthesized by considering the collaboration of the lateral and vertical heterostructure. CDs stitched together seamlessly in-plane of multi-vesicle sulfur-doped CN tubes via covalent bonds at the interface (denoted as 7CDs/SMVCTs), allowing the lateral heterostructure with one-photon excitation pathway to effectively separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs and adjust the energy band structure of CN. Simultaneously, ultrathin Nipc is highly dispersed on 7CDs/SMVCTs by the surface hydroxyl-induced assembly, constructing the vertical heterostructure with a two-photon excitation pathway, extending light absorption and accelerating the reduction reaction kinetics. Advanced characterization techniques and DFT calculations unveiled that the CDs and Ni–N4 as active sites in 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % photocatalyst were responsible for H+ reduction. Consequently, the 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (65,454.47 μmol g−1) displays superior photocatalytic H2 performance, with a generation 23.2 times higher than PCN (2816.90 μmol g−1) after 4 hours. This study provides new insight into rational preparation of the bidirectional heterojunctions in CN-based photocatalysts.

Typically, the molar ratio of melamine and thiocyanuric acid is 1:1 and dissolved in deionized water (50 mL) with 600 rpm stirring, simultaneously adding a proper amount of γ-CD, after continuous stirring for 24 h, followed by freeze-drying. Finally, the as-obtained faint yellow powder was transferred to a crucible with a cover and heated from ambient to 550 °C for 4 h in the N2 atmosphere. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the obtained dark product was naturally cooled to ambient temperature and denoted as xCDs/SMVCTs. Similarly, xCDs/SMVCTs (x = 0, 3, 5, 7, and 10 mg) were produced with the variation in the amount of γ-CD. The CSCN was prepared by directly heating melamine,thiocyanuric acid, and γ-CD under the same procedure. The pristine CN (denoted as PCN) was obtained through typical thermal polycondensation.

A proper amount of product was added to 50 mL of ethanol under ultrasonication for 15 min, then 0.5 mg mL−1 of Nipc solution (ethanol as solvent) was added, followed by the sonication for 15 min, further stirred at room temperature for 24 h then get the solvent wholly evaporated under vigorous stirring at 80 °C. The sample was named 7CDs/SMVCTs/Niy, where y (0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 %) is the mass ratio percentage of Nipc to 7CDs/SMVCTs.

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was performed under simulated solar irradiation by a 300 W xenon lamp (CELPE300L-3 A, cut by 1.5 A M filter, Perfectlight Limited, Beijing) in a closed circulating system. Generally, 5 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL aqueous solution (10 vol% TEOA), and 1.0 wt% Pt cocatalyst was by an in situ photoreduction method H2PtCl6. The hydrogen was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC2014C) using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD), a 5 Å molecular sieve column with high-purity argon carrier gas. The AQY for H2 evolution was measured with a 380 nm, 400 nm, and 420 nm cutoff filter, and the active area of the reactor was ≈15.98 cm2. The AQY was estimated as follows:

mml:mrow.mml:mi: the number of the incident photon

ce:section.ce:para:
The photoelectrochemical measurements were investigated with a standard three-electrode system in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution (pH = 7.0) utilizing a CHI 660e electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai). Platinum plate as the counter electrode, FTO glass (1 cm × 2 cm) as the working electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The working electrode prepared 10 mg photocatalysts were dispersed in 3 mL ethanol with 15 μL Nafion and sonicated for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension. The slurry was then coated onto an FTO glass and dried at 100 °C. The potential was converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) via a Nernst equation:
E (RHE) = E (Ag/AgCl) + 0.059 × pH + 0.197 V

The 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % photocatalyst was prepared by in situ supramolecular self-assembly, thermal polymerization of freeze-dried supramolecular aggregates, and the surface hydroxyl group-induced assembly (detailed synthesis procedure provided in the experimental section) ( Fig. 1a). As is well known, γ-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) as a structure-directing agent can control morphology and as carbon precursors can be converted to carbon dots via annealing. Noticeably, γ-CD has a cavity structure and hydroxyl groups at the external side, which construct unique architecture via regulating the intermolecular interactions with other molecules. Therefore, using melamine, thiocyanuric acid and γ-CD as the raw material, xCDs/SMVCTs were fabricated by the H-bonding-induced self-assembling process, freeze-drying and calcination [29]. Through the surface hydroxyl group-induced assembly, the small amounts of Nipc were uniformly dispersed on the surface of the 7CDs/SMVCTs, constructing vertical heterostructure [25,26,30]. As a result, the unique 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % bidirectional heterostructure is in situ synthesized as a promising candidate for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution by bidirectional electron transfer channels.

The as-synthesized heterostructure has multivesicular CN tubes with several micron lengths, which differs from the PCN morphology (Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1b-d) show that the as-synthesized 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % heterostructure has multi-vesicle tubes. The volumetric comparison images of CSCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs indicate supramolecular self-assembly and γ-CD play a vital role in controlling morphology (Fig. S2). A close observation of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (Fig. 1e), the formation of numerous nanopores in the multi-vesicle tubes can occur via gas shock (such as CO2, H2S and NH3), structural collapse and self-template (γ-CD and thiocyanuric acid) during the pyrolysis process. The sizes of these nanopores range from tens to hundreds of nanometers. Simultaneously, large nanopores and the ultrathin (16 nm, indicated by the white dotted line) nanovesicle wall will offer accessible routes and a short diffusion distance for electrons, consistent with pore size analysis (Fig. S3). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were performed to identify the existence of CDs (Fig. 1f, g), revealing that the right amounts and small size of CDs are randomly anchored on the multivesicular, indicating γ-CD is derived into CDs under thermal process, and the covalent interactions forming lateral heterostructure between SMVCTs and CDs [7,9,17,31]. Additionally, a comparable line scan of the chosen red region (Fig. 1i) and the HR-TEM image in Fig. 1h demonstrate well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.202 nm, which corresponds closely with the (101) facet in graphitic carbon [7]. The ultrasmall of CDs embedded in the SMVCTs skeleton could expose many active sites and accelerate the charge transfer at the interface, thus increasing its catalytic activity. EDS-elemental mapping images show that C, N, O, S, and Ni elements were evenly dispersed on 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % material (Fig. 1j). Meanwhile, Fig. 1k shows an aberration-corrected HAADF-STEM image of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, in which bright spots are evenly dispersed on the surface of the 7CDs/SMVCTs, which corresponds to single Ni atoms or cluster. Combined with the above results, one can conclude that Nipc is well-dispersed on the surface of 7CDs/SMVCTs as molecular photocatalysts [26].

The basic physical characterization experiments were conducted to study how much γ-CD affects the specific surface area and multi-level pore structure. As displayed in Fig. 2a, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area value (Fig. S3a-f) of PCN and xCDs/SMVCTs series (x = 0, 3, 5, 7, 10) are detected to be about 9.91, 60.75, 79.66, 80.07, 74.49, and 69.80 m2 g−1, respectively, and the corresponding pore size (Fig. S3g-l) is 32.45, 31.54, 30.57, 30.13, 30.01, and 28.87 nm, and mesoporous volume decreased step by step. The carbonization of γ-CD is derived into CDs, and the mesoporous volume and pore diameter decrease with the increase of CDs in the in-plane SMVCTs skeleton. The Raman spectra could verify the existence of Nipc and graphite carbon structure. As shown in Fig. 2b, the sp2 graphite carbon atoms' vibrations are associated with the G band detected around 1461 cm−1, other than the disordered and defective sp3 carbon atoms' vibrations are linked to the D band at roughly 1313 cm−1 [25,32]. Increasing the γ-CD content boosts the G band signal. The considerable growth of sp2-hybridized π-conjugated CDs in contrast to periodic units of tri-s-triazine units in PCN demonstrates the expansion of CDs content in the xCDs/SMVCTs [7,9]. The macrocycle (684.4 cm−1) and pyrrole (1137.8 cm−1) breathing vibrations of Nipc are sensitive to intermolecular interactions[33]. For 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (Fig. 2c), the bands of PCN remain unchanged, and sensitive bands of Nipc nearly disappear, suggesting the reduced intermolecular interaction of Nipc molecules. The bands are relevant to C-N-C in the Nipc molecule for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % noticeably move to ≈1561.3 cm−1, as opposed to 1546.6 cm−1 for pure Nipc, indicating that the interfacial interaction significantly may cause a change in the electron density of C–N to varying degrees [33,34].

As seen in Fig. 2d1, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were conducted to analyze the crystal structure of the PCN, xCDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Niy. The two distinct peaks of PCN at 27.4° and 13.2° are consistent with those of traditional CN, which can correspond to the π-π interlayer stacking (002) and tri-s-triazine unit (100) planes, respectively [35–38]. The XRD spectra for those xCDs/SMVCTs heterojunction show profiles comparable to PCN, indicating that PCN's original crystal structure is retained even after incorporating CDs. The (100) characteristic peaks of xCDs/SMVCTs heterojunction were weakened (Fig. 2d2), which would be related to the in-plane structure's broken periodicity because of the porous structures of the SMVCTs, which is comparable to the phenomenon of porous CN nanosheets that has been documented [39]. The (002) diffraction peak of xCDs/SMVCTs is broad, weak and slightly negatively shifted compared with the PCN, suggesting the addition of CDs has increased the interlayer spacing and reduced the thickness of the nanovesicle wall [9,40,41]. Otherwise, because of the small quantity and high dispersion of Nipc, none of the typical diffraction peaks from Nipc emerge in the 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % patterns (Fig. 2e).

As shown in Fig. 2 f1, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of xCDs/SMVCTs are in agreement with the conventional vibration modes of PCN, revealing that the incorporation of CDs into SMVCTs does not alter the chemical structure of PCN. The solid characteristic sharp band at 812 cm–1 relates to the intrinsic heptazine unit of the carbon nitride structure [42,43]. Notably, this stretched vibrations band periodically negatively shifts in xCDs/SMVCTs, reflecting the existence of CDs and a significant interaction at the heterointerface between SMVCTs and CDs (Fig. 2 f2). The asymmetric peaks at 1200–1700 cm–1 are from the stretching mode of N-containing heterocycles [44–46]. The broad absorption bands at 3000–3500 cm–1 are associated with N-H/O-H stretching vibrations of the surface -NH/-OH/NH2 groups [7,47]. Notably, the broadened hydroxyl band at 3400 cm−1 could be interpreted as a sign that rich hydroxyl group CDs from the pyrolysis of γ-CD have been incorporated into SMVCTs [17]. There are no discernible vibrations that match C-O groups, which might indicate that the vibrations are being overlapped by that of the C-N bond or low CDs content. For 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, no characteristic peaks assigned to Nipc are observed because of the tiny amounts of Nipc. In contrast, the peaks of 7CDs/SMVCTs are well retained, proving the loaded Nipc interacts with 7CDs/SMVCTs via the noncovalent bonding (Fig. 2g). Also, the surface hydroxyl peak intensity of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % is significantly weakened, implying the chemical connection of Nipc with the surface hydroxyl groups of 7CDs/SMVCTs. Therefore, Raman, XRD, and FT-IR results are consistent with the hypothesis of H-bonding between the H atom of -OH and the N atom of Nipc ligand.

To confirm the successful introduction of CDs and loading of Nipc, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the surface elemental compositions of the PCN, 7CDs/SMVCTs, and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %. Both PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs are mainly composed of carbon and nitrogen, the C/N atomic ratio is increased from 0.76 to 0.79 (Table S1). Enriching the C element in tubular 7CDs/SMVCTs suggests that CDs have been successfully embedded into the CN skeleton frame. The high-resolution C 1 s binding energy of lateral 7CDs/SMVCTs heterojunction can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 284.8 eV (C-C/CC), 286.0 eV (CN), 288.1 eV (sp2 hybrid carbon within the C3N4 skeleton, C-NC), respectively (Fig. S5b) [43,48,49]. A peak at 288.8 eV emerged on 7CDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, corresponding to C-O, owing to the surface hydroxylation of CDs and adsorbed oxygen-containing groups (Fig. S5b and Fig. 3a) [50,51]. However, for PCN, 288.7 eV is attributed to the surface oxides (Fig. S5a) [7,52]. Moreover, the C-NC peak exhibits a slight shift to lower direction movement (288.1 eV) due to anion doping sites replacing the adjacent nitrogen atoms of N-CN [53]. The four binding energy peaks of N 1 s are observed as 398.7 eV (sp2-coordinated nitrogen, CN-C), 400.0 eV (bridging N atoms in N-(C)3/H-N-(C)2 groups), 401.2 eV (amino functions carrying hydrogen, C-N-H) and 404.2 eV (charge effect or π excitation), respectively (Fig. S5d) [43,54–56]. The binding energies of C-NC, N-(C)3/H-N-(C)2, and C-N-H in 7CDs/SMVCTs are shifted to the low position, compared with PCN (Fig. S5c). The shifts of element-binding energy could reflect the changes in electron density, which indicates that the electronic structure and surface chemical environment of SMVCTs change after coupling CDs. For the O 1 s spectrum, two peaks are detected in 7CDs/SMVCTs heterojunction, ascribed to the CO and the C atoms-bonded surface hydroxyl (C-O-H) groups, respectively (Fig S5f) [47,57,58]. For PCN (Fig. S5e), two signal peaks could be ascribed to the adsorbed oxygen species, such as water and oxygen [57,58]. Furthermore, the same peak position is found in S 2p (163.4 eV) spectrum of 7CDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %. It indicated the sulfur-doped in carbon nitride skeleton, consistent with the EDS results (Fig. 3c).

As shown in Fig. 3d, the Ni 2p spectrum of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % could be divided into two peaks, indicating that the 855.8 and 873.2 eV peaks belonged to Ni2+. In contrast to pure Nipc, the binding energies of Ni 2p in 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % are unshifted, which proves that the surface hydroxyl groups lead to interfacial interaction promoting dispersion of Nipc assemblies and do not affect the chemical states of Ni species [23]. To further assess the electronic structure and local environment of Ni centers, the Ni K-edge X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectra for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, Nipc and Ni-foil were collected (Fig. 3e). The yellow line peak of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % at 8350 eV is higher than that of Ni foil, indicating the cationic electronic state of single-atomic-site Ni in 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % system [59,60]. The signal peaks at approximately 8344–8340 eV are mainly originated from the centrosymmetric Ni-N4 center of Nipc and square-planar [61,62]. By comparison, the peak position of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % shifts slightly to lower energy, indicating charge transfer between Nipc and the 7CDs/SMVCTs. The similar pre-edge profile between 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % and Nipc, the prominent peak at around 1.5 Å, which was attributed to the scattering interaction between the Ni atoms and the first shell (Ni–N) (Fig. 3f) and close to the Ni–N bonds in the standard Nipc sample[63,64], where a single Ni atom is coordinated with four surrounding N atoms. Furthermore, no Ni−Ni bond at about 2.16 Å can be detected, further manifesting the atomic dispersion of Ni species on the 7CDs/SMVCTs surface. To sum up, no prominent peaks for longer backscattering paths were observed in FT-EXAFS spectra of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, suggesting a uniform dispersion of Nipc on the 7CDs/SMVCTs [65,66]. Due to the multivesicular tubes with curved structure, 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 shows a slightly elongated Ni−N bond, resulting in the peak slightly shifted [67,68]. The Fourier-transformed EXAFS (FT-EXAFS) magnitudes at Ni K-edge in q-space show that the oscillation resolution of Nipc is dispersed on 7CDs/SMVCTs (Fig. 3g). The FT-EXAFS spectrum of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % was well fitted in R space using backscattering paths of Ni–N. The fitting results are shown in Table S2. The coordination number of Ni–N was about 4, which exhibited that the configuration of Ni−N first shell coordinated with about four N atoms in 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 (Fig. 3h). This number is reasonable as the XAS signal is an average probing of the single-atom sites in the catalyst [65,69]. It is concluded that the chemical structure of Nipc is well-preserved and stable after being loaded on 7CDs/SMVCTs. These results indicated that ultrathin Nipc is dispersed on 7CDs/SMVCTs by the surface hydroxyl-induced assembly unchanged the chemical states of Ni centers, agreeing well with the XPS Ni 2p spectra and corresponding with the H-bonding connection mode at the interface.

Optimizing the morphology and modification in the surface results in the recombination of electrons and holes are conspicuously inhibited for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, as revealed by the significantly weakened photoluminescence (PL) peak ( Fig. 4a). Sufficient light absorption is an essential prerequisite for photocatalytic H2 evolution. For lateral heterostructure, the PL signals of 7CDs/SMVCTs progressively weaken and blue-shift (Fig. S6a). The former indicates the prevented recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and promotes the efficient separation of photoinduced carriers, and the latter is associated with optimizing the electronic structures of SMVCTs. With increasing the incorporated amount of Nipc, the signal becomes weaker than that of other samples and reaches the low for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % with the best charge separation (Fig. S6b). Simultaneously, the as-produced amount of hydroxyl radical under illumination for the samples could be examined by fluorescence spectra (FS) and proportional reflect the charge separation [23,70]. As shown in Fig. S7, the charge separation of PCN is greatly improved by introducing appropriate amounts of CDs and Nipc. Therefore, FS and PL spectra co-confirm the photogenerated charge properties of xCDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Niy heterojunctions. Adding CDs and Nipc to SMVCTs will significantly alter the optical properties of the bidirectional heterojunctions. In agreement with the published study, the absorption spectra of PCN display a representative absorption band with an extrapolated onset of approximately 450 nm [38,71,72]. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, for PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs, 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % exhibits significantly higher absorption intensity. Excitingly, the absorption edge of xCDs/SMVCTs shows apparent redshift and the enhanced band-tail associated absorption band spanning from 500 to 780 nm (Fig. S8a) [73,74]. The synergistic effect of CDs and numerous reflections of incident light within the multi-vesicle framework imply the intimate contact of SMVCTs and CDs.

The bandgaps of these samples were determined by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy [75]. Bandgaps of xCDs/SMVCTs decrease from 2.54 eV to 2.16 eV (Fig. S8b), confirming that the incorporation of CDs can shrink the band gap as the strong interaction between SMVCTs and CDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, bandgaps of PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs also shrink gradually with the construction of lateral heterojunctions, which are 2.61 eV and 2.25 eV, respectively. Electrochemical M-S and the VB XPS spectra experiments were performed to determine the relative positions of CB and VB edges (Figs. S9 and S10) [32,76]. The CB of the 7CDs/SMVCTs (−0.74 eV) shifts negatively to 0.18 eV compared with that of PCN (−0.56 eV) (Fig. 4d). The VB XPS analysis of PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs demonstrates that S-doping and CDs results in a more negative position of the CB and a smaller bandgap. It leads to an increased thermodynamic driving force in the photocatalytic reduction of hydrogen protons and the extension of optical absorption [77,78]. 7CDs/SMVCTs has a greater VB level than PCN, with a 0.54 V increase from 2.05 to 1.51 V. The VB edge upshift trend is also detected in the VB XPS of PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs. The observed upshift from 2.13 to 1.57 eV by 0.56 eV approach 0.54 eV (Fig. 4e). These results indicate that the introduction of CDs and S-doping promote a thermodynamically driving force for reducing the protons to H2. The positions of the CB and VB can be changed after the establishment of lateral heterostructure and achieve a one-photon excitation pathway (Fig. S11).

Furthermore, the UVvis absorption spectroscopy shows that the characteristic absorption of Nipc is gradually enhanced with the increase in the amount of introduced Nipc, indicating that the polymeric Nipc are successfully modified on the 7CDs/SMVCTs (Fig. S12). The LUMO level of Nipc is estimated to be −0.59 eV based on the M–S curve and UVvis absorption spectroscopy data (Fig. S13). Although the CB of the 7CDs/SMVCTs is observably higher than that of Nipc, the introduction of Nipc can also absorb the light spectrum in the range of 550−750 nm and provide Ni-N4 active sites, which contributes to enhancing visible-light response-ability and photocatalytic H2 evolution activity [75,77]. The 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % structure possesses lateral and vertical heterostructure caused by supramolecular self-assembly and the surface hydroxyl group-induced (Fig. 4g). Moreover, the reduction of electron transfer resistance and a noticeable enhancement of photocurrent for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Nipc further demonstrate enhancement of charge dynamics, the improved separation of photogenerated carriers, and visible-light response-ability (Fig. 4f, h and Fig. S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism and performance of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %
ce:section.ce:para: Light-assisted KPFM is employed to probe the change in the local surface potential of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % in darkness and under light irradiation, which can indicate photogenerated electrons at the interface of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % bidirectional heterojunctions [78]. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images and corresponding height distribution curves ( Fig. 5a, d). Compared with that of the "dark-state" (Fig. 5b, e), the surface potential of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % exhibits increases from 169.68 to 176.39 mV and from 95.22 to 173.34 mV when exposed to irradiation, indirectly confirming that there is the dynamic transfer of photogenerated electrons between the interfaces of bidirectional heterojunctions (Fig. 5c, f). Simultaneously, the surface potential of 7CDs/SMVCTs and SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % show increases from 35.26 to 60.18 mV and from 0 to 48.34 mV when exposed to irradiation (Fig. S15), further demonstrating that construction of heterojunction contributes to photogenerated electrons transfer.

Transient absorption spectroscopy (TA) is performed at 400 nm excitation to probe the dynamics of the photogenerated carriers to understand the intrinsic photocatalytic dynamics and charge transfer pathways in water [79,80]. Upon light excitation, all samples show continuous and wideband absorption within the probe light range of 470–650 nm (Fig. S16). The broad negative absorption band was observed for samples, mainly due to the ground state bleaching (GSB) [81]. The GBS with negative signal is situated within the 470–650 nm wavelength range, originating from neutral singlet excitons in PCN (Fig. 5g) [82,83]. The introduced in-plane CDs and vertical Nipc in SMVCTs induce the unique electron trap state, which greatly influences the charge dynamics (Fig. 5h, i). The decay of the GSB signal verified that the GSB signal is mainly contributed to by photogenerated electrons. Compared to PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs, the signal intensity for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % gradually increased demonstrating lots of charges are generated in bidirectional heterojunctions (Fig. 5i). The dynamical decay traces of the photo-excited carriers of PCN, 7CDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % were plotted and probed at 520 nm and fitted the TA kinetic curves by the three-exponential function, as shown in Fig. 5j, k, l. For PCN, 7CDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, the τ1 and τ2 correspond to the lifetime of fast decay due to trapping rates of carriers by trap states, while τ3 corresponds to the lifetime of slow due to the recombination time of carriers. Based on the formula, the highest is the τ3 value of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (7460 ps). This indicates that as-prepared 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % catalysts have the longest lifetime of carriers, in which long-lived photogenerated electrons are primarily responsible for the substantial improvement of hydrogen production activity.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculation further explains the introduction of CDs and Nipc effect on the band gap and charge behavior of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %. The unit structures of PCN, 7CDs/SMVCTs, and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % were optimized, calculating their electronic structure. The density of states (DOS) of PCN was shown in Fig. 6a, in comparison with those of the 7CDs/SMVCTs (Fig. 6b) and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (Fig. 6c). The estimated band gap values of PCN (1.73 eV), 7CDs/SMVCTs (1.56 eV), and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % (0.98 eV) are smaller than the experimental value, owning to the well-known limitations of DFT calculations [32,84]. Consequently, this conclusion may still indicate that the band gap of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % is narrower than that of PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs, which is commensurate with the above experimental findings. Notably, with the progressive introduction of CDs and Nipc, the substantial electron density is dispersed around the Fermi level. This electronic property with many mobile electrons around the Fermi level would significantly increase the adsorption energy of water molecules and reduce the interfacial energy barrier of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % during the photocatalytic H2 evolution process [85]. Then, the work function after coupling the two-phase and three-phase structure is calculated to probe the transport of carriers (Fig. 6d-f). Compared with PCN (4.30 eV), the electrons of 7CDs/SMVCTs (4.06 eV) were kept horizontal, which did not require excess electron-balancing Fermi energy, indicating the inherent superiority of the structure. In contrast to 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % heterostructure (4.03 eV), lateral and vertical heterostructures can serve as the transport pathways of charge that would accelerate the catalytic reaction kinetics process. Thus, bidirectional heterojunctions require small amounts of energy in catalytic reactions to improve the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 6 g shows the Gibbs free energy difference (ΔGH*) between different possible active sites on phase structure and gaseous H2 for evaluating the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. Compared with PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs, the CDs and Ni-N4 active sites on 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % exhibit the lowest ΔGH* value (1.770 eV and −2.569 eV, respectively).

Furthermore, the differential charge density map of the 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % with dual active sites photocatalyst can be observed in Fig. 6 h, i. When carbon dots are utilized as active sites, there is a tendency for electrons to collect at the CDs sites. The planar average charge difference suggests that lateral transportation of electrons occurs from the SMVCTs to the CDs. In addition, the introduction of Nipc in 7CDs/SMVCTs results in the attraction of electrons to Ni-N4 sites, leading to the formation of electron concentration zones. The planar average charge difference indicates vertical electron transfer from the SMVCTs to Ni-N4. Notably, the electrons mainly located on the surface of CDs and Ni-N4 are primarily sourced from SMVCTs, providing more evidence for the effective formation of bidirectional heterojunction. The electron concentration zones covering both the plane and axial direction suggest that photoexcited electrons may move across these areas, acting as a bidirectional electronic channel. The corresponding planar average charge density difference demonstrates a considerable enhancement in the model's separation of charge carriers in both the plane and axial direction.

The theoretical simulation provides strong evidence that the co-attachment of CDs and Ni-N4 on SMVCTs may efficiently capture a more significant number of photoexcited electrons, boosting their participation in the H+ reduction process. A hypothesized mechanism for the photocatalytic hydrogen development over the 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % is presented based on combining detailed characterizations with theoretical modeling. As shown in Scheme 1, when 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % are exposed to the whole solar light spectrum, a significant number of photoexcited electron-hole pairs are produced. The gradual separation and transit dynamics of charge carriers on SMVCTs are significantly improved due to the following variables. It contributes to the generation of long-lived free photogenerated electrons, resulting in a notable enhancement in photocatalytic activity (TEOA quenches holes). On the one hand, CDs modification induced local electric field form lateral heterojunction, effectively facilitating the separation and transport of electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, the vertical heterojunction created in 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % serves as another electron transfer channel, accelerating the transportation of electrons between the SMVCTs and Nipc, leading to lots of electrons being captured by Ni-N4. The strengthened charge carrier separation and transport dynamics on SMVCTs result in better photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity, attributed to the significant contribution of CDs and Ni-N4.

The optimized 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % shows a photocatalytic H2 production rate of 16,363.62 μmol g−1 h−1, which by far exceeds that of PCN (704.23 μmol g−1 h−1) and 7CDs/SMVCTs (6876.38 μmol g−1 h−1) ( Fig. 7b). We also test the photocatalytic H2 production rate over a different amount of γ-CD and different mass ratios of Nipc (Fig. S17). The different loading amounts of Pt and the amount of catalyst are also optimized to obtain the best performance (Fig. S18). As depicted in Fig. 7c, the AQY values of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % at various wavelengths strongly correlate with the light absorption trend, indicating that the H2 evolution process is predominantly photoinduced. The AQY at 380 reaches 7.08 % (Table S3). Meanwhile, the cycling H2 evolution performances of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % exhibit refreshable photocatalytic activities (Fig. 7d). The sample was characterized by TEM and XRD after five cycling tests. Fig. S19 shows well-preserved morphology and crystalline structure, indicating that the 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % photocatalyst exhibits excellent structural stability. As shown in Fig. S20, comparing wavelength-dependent H2 generation of PCN, 7CDs/SMVCTs and 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % reveal the relationship between extended light absorption and photocatalytic performance. For the PCN sample, little H2 generation occurs when the incident light is above the wavelength of λ ≥ 450 nm. By comparison, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % is 2374.35 μmol g−1 h−1 and 51.48 μmol g−1 h−1 under irradiation with light λ ≥ 450 nm and λ ≥ 600 nm, respectively, which is significantly longer than the absorption edge of PCN. This result confirms that the addition of CDs and Nipc contributes to improving light absorption and utilization.

Remarkably, for the bidirectional heterojunctions of 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 %, the electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (EPR) signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − are further prominent strengthened and weakened in comparison with those of PCN and 7CDs/SMVCTs, respectively (Fig. 7e, f). The main reasons were that the oxidation potentials of VB of Nipc were more positive than that of H2O/•OH potential, while the reduction potentials of CB of 7CDs/SMVCTs were more negative than that of O2/•O2 − potential. The former is that Nipc is excited by ≥600 nm light, the photogenerated electrons would transfer from Nipc to 7CDs/SMVCTs. The latter is that a mass of photogenerated electrons accumulated on the CDs and Ni-N4 under simulated sunlight irradiation, to which the bidirectional charge migration mechanism should contribute. One-photon and two-photon excitation routes can work in concert to suppress recombination of the photoinduced carriers and boost efficient charge transfer for 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % [17,23]. Based on theoretical calculations and energy band relationships, it is postulated that the heterojunction exhibits excitation pathways for both single and two photons. Under light irradiation, photoexcited electrons for PCN are transported from the VB to the CB, rest in the VB, and then recombine with the photoexcited holes of PCN (Fig. 7gI). By one-photon excitation pathway, the photoexcited electrons in CB of SMVCTs will transfer CDs within the lateral heterostructure of 7CDs/SMVCTs (Fig. 7gII). The LUMO level of Nipc is estimated to be −0.59 eV (Fig. S13). This value is marginally lower than the CB bottom level of 7CDs/SMVCTs. Thankfully, the photoelectrons will migrate from SMVCTs to the ligand of the Nipc and eventually to the single Ni2+ sites. Meanwhile, the photoexcited electrons in LUMO of Nipc will migrate to the ligand of Nipc and then to the single Ni2+ sites, which form a two-photon excitation pathway (Fig. 7gIII). In conclusion, it has been shown that the photoinduced electrons primarily reside on the surface of CDs and Ni-N4, augmenting the impetus for hydrogen proton reduction and consequently enhancing the performance of photocatalytic hydrogen generation.

In summary, we have effectively constructed bidirectional heterojunctions with CDs and Nipc co-modified sulfur-doped multivesicular CN tubes. This structure resulted in a bidirectional transfer mechanism that can promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. As-prepared 7CDs/SMVCTs/Ni0.5 % catalyst shows an impressive photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 16,363.62 μmol g−1 h−1, with a rate 23.2 times that of PCN (704.23 μmol g−1 h−1). The enhancement of photocatalytic performance is induced by dual reduction sites and one/two-photon excitation pathways, which can increase photon utilization, improve spatial carrier transport and prevent photogenic electron-hole pairs recombination. This work provides new strategic insights into rationally designing other bidirectional heterojunctions with dual reduction sites.

Xuan Miao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Peng Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bolong Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Xiao Bai: Formal analysis. Weisheng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
